results.possibleIdentity: Olive Freud (born 1936) is a New York-based mathematician, educator, and prominent quality-of-life activist. She is best known for her role as the president of the Committee for Environmentally Sound Development, a non-profit organization based on the Upper West Side of Manhattan that advocates against overdevelopment and "gerrymandered" zoning lots. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Freud was an active technical participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which fostered collaborations between the arts and scientific communities. In 1968, she collaborated with architect and designer Don Edson (born 1934) on a project featured in the landmark E.A.T. exhibition titled "Some More Beginnings" held at the Brooklyn Museum. Professionally, she is a retired mathematics teacher and has been associated with Columbia University in the fields of design and mathematics. She has lived in the same rent-controlled apartment on West 72nd Street since the 1960s with her husband, Edgar Freud, who is a retired engineer. In 2023, she was honored with the Clara Lemlich Award for her decades of social and environmental activism in New York City.
